docs.title: Feudalism 4
docs.text:
Release 4.1.0 — personal inventory, multi-pouch play, and starter-character onboarding.

A medieval-fantasy roleplay game system for Second Life. Build a character, earn experience, fight and hunt, gather and farm, and master a dozen crafting professions — all driven by a single wearable HUD.

This guide is for players learning the game and for universe admins and item owners running and expanding it.

Get Started Browse the HUD

docs.text:

docs.title: Explore by topic
docs.text:
• Your Character — the HUD, stats, vitals, XP, species and classes, modes and resting.
  • Inventory & Storage — personal inventory, pouches, worn tools, chests, and coins.
  • Living off the Land — foraging, mining, hunting, farming, livestock, and beekeeping.
  • Crafting & Professions — cooking, smithing, leatherworking, brewing, alchemy, and milling.
  • Recipes — how to use recipes, and how to author new ones.

docs.title: Join the Realm Economy
docs.text:
The Feudalism 4 Trading Guild

Feudalism 4 is backed by a living ecosystem. Join the official Trading Guild in Second Life to physically meet with fellow players, showcase what your character has gathered or built, coordinate market values, and barter for rare materials. It's the economic beating heart of the community.

Your universe sets the rules

Feudalism 4 is a system, not a story. Each region (universe) decides its own rules for combat consent, death, theft, magic, and economy — and can enable, disable, or filter features. When this guide and your universe's rules disagree, your universe's rules win. When in doubt, ask staff.

docs.title: For Universe Admins
docs.text:
A universe is your world — a region or group of regions sharing characters, rules, species, classes, and an economy. As an admin you decide what Feudalism 4 looks like for your players.

  • Universes, Species & Classes — what a universe is and how you curate its options.
  • Players, XP & Economy — supporting players, awarding XP, and running the coin economy.

System Admins vs Universe Admins

Universe (sim) admins run a world: rules, events, XP/coin awards, and which features are on. System admins manage the underlying templates — species, classes, and global data shared across universes. This section focuses on the universe-admin role.

docs.title: Supporting players
docs.text:
Most player problems are one of a few things — keep this triage handy:

Symptom Likely cause Fix "Nothing saves." Experience permission declined. Re-wear HUD, accept the popup. "Objects don't work." No character loaded, or HUD not attached. Open Setup HUD, load/create a character. "My stats are stale." Region change without refresh. Touch Sync/Update on the HUD. "Can't craft." Missing/misspelled ingredients. Check exact lowercase slugs and quantities. "Can't use that object." Security set to owner/group. Adjust the object's security or grant access.

docs.title: XP awards
docs.text:
Players earn XP automatically from combat, crafting, harvesting, and training (see XP & Available Points). As an admin you may also award XP for events and roleplay through your build's admin tools. Remember the conversion: 1,000 XP = 1 Available Point.

Tune difficulty with Challenge Rating

The CR you set on crafting and harvesting objects is your main XP/economy dial — higher CR means more XP per success but more failures and longer timers. Keep CRs consistent across similar activities so progression feels fair.

docs.title: Running the economy
docs.text:
Currency (copper, silver, gold) lives on each character's record — never as gatherable items.

  • Payouts come from in-world paychest objects you configure (wages, stipends, bounties) and from direct admin awards.
  • Player-to-player payments use the HUD Coins → Give Coins flow within 10 m.
  • Vaults let players deposit/withdraw when one is nearby.

Protect the money supply

Gatherable scripts cannot create coin (gold coin, silver coin, copper coin are blocked). Keep it that way: route all coin through paychests, vaults, and admin tools so you control inflation.

docs.title: Economy design checklist
docs.text:
• Decide your item slug list and keep it canonical — see Item Slug Conventions.
  • Set gatherable yields and cooldowns to control raw-material supply.
  • Set recipe CRs to balance effort vs reward.
  • Choose coin sinks (taxes, vendor prices, repairs) so coin leaves circulation.
  • Place storage and vendor objects with sensible security.
  • Publish your local rules (consent, death, theft, trade) where players can find them.

docs.title: What a universe is
docs.text: A universe is the container for your world's characters and rules. Players pick a universe when they create a character (some universes require a registration code you provide). A player can hold different characters in different universes, and their personal inventory follows each character.

docs.title: What you control
docs.text:
Lever Effect Available species Curate which species players may choose at creation. Available classes Curate the class list and their stat caps. Available genders Limit or allow the full gender list. Mana rules Whether magic/mana is in play, and which species can cast. Feature toggles Enable/disable combat, currency, specific stations, passive stamina drain, etc. Roleplay rules Consent, death, capture, theft, and economy policy (your story, not the system's).

Curate for tone

A gritty low-fantasy universe might allow only Human, Elf, and Dwarf with no mana; a high-fantasy one might open every species and class. The system gives you the full catalog — you decide the menu.

docs.title: Species & classes (the catalog)
docs.text: The base game ships a large catalog (many species; over a hundred classes). Each species defines default stats, minimums, maximums, and a mana chance; each class defines per-stat caps. Players can never exceed a class cap or the absolute maximum of 9. See the player-facing Species, Classes & Genders page for how this looks to them.

docs.title: Admin tools
docs.text:
Admin functions appear on the Setup HUD → Admin tab, visible only to accounts with admin access. Use it for the universe-management tasks your build exposes (player lookups, awards, and configuration).

Document your local rules

This guide explains the system. Your players still need your universe's rules for consent, death, economy, and events. Publish those alongside this guide and link them from your region.

docs.title: Your Character
docs.text:
Everything about you as a player lives here: the HUD you wear, the editor you use to build your character, your stats and vitals, and how you grow over time.

  • The Players HUD — the worn HUD and what every sphere and button does.
  • The Setup HUD — the web panel where you build and edit your character.
  • Stats & Vitals — the 20 attributes, and how health/stamina/mana are calculated.
  • XP & Available Points — how you earn XP and spend it to raise stats.
  • Species, Classes & Genders — your starting line, caps, and options.
  • Game Modes & Resting — roleplay, tournament, OOC, AFK, and recovery.
  • The Meter — the optional floating name/title/bars display.

docs.title: The Meter
docs.text: The Meter is an optional attachment that shows floating text above your head — your name, title, mode, and (optionally) your vital bars. It's a display only: it mirrors what your Players HUD sends and stores no data of its own.

docs.title: What it shows
docs.text:
• Your character name and title.
  • Your current mode marker (roleplay, OOC, AFK, tournament, ★).
  • Optionally, your Health / Stamina / Mana bars.

docs.title: Turning bars on or off
docs.text: Open the Setup HUD → Options tab and toggle Show Bars. In Tournament mode the bars are always shown for fairness.

docs.title: Do you need it?
docs.text:
No — the Meter is convenience and immersion. Other players can read your name, title, and status at a glance, which is handy for roleplay. Your HUD works fine without it.

Wear it like any attachment

Attach the Feudalism 4 Meter from your inventory. If the text looks stale, touch Sync/Update on your Players HUD to refresh what the Meter is mirroring.

docs.title: Game Modes & Resting
docs.text: Your HUD has a few modes that change how you're treated in play, plus a rest system for recovering health, stamina, and mana.

docs.title: Game modes
docs.text:
Set your mode on the Setup HUD → Options tab (some HUD rest menus also switch it).

Mode What it means for you Roleplay The default. Full stats, XP, combat, and crafting are active. Tournament PvP-balanced: combat stats are treated as 5 for everyone, with fixed display pools, so fights are about skill, not character power. The Meter always shows bars. OOC Out of character. The Meter shows an OOC marker. Leaving roleplay for OOC saves your pools and XP. AFK Away from keyboard. The Meter shows an AFK marker. None / ★ Hides the Meter text.

Switching modes saves your data

Leaving roleplay mode (for OOC, AFK, or tournament) triggers a save of your XP and pools — a good habit before you log off.

docs.title: Resting & recovery
docs.text:
Open the rest menu with the Rest (heart) button on your HUD.

IC RestOOC ResetStop Resting

In-character rest. Your character sits, and every 5 seconds you regain:

  • +1 Health
  • +1 Stamina
  • +1 Mana (if you have mana)

Resting stops automatically when you're fully recovered, or when you choose Stop Resting (which also saves your data).

Instantly refills health, stamina, and mana to maximum. It's an out-of-character convenience for between scenes — not an in-character healing effect.

Ends a rest early. Do this before traveling or fighting so you're not stuck in the rest animation.

Player steps — recover after a fight

  1. Get somewhere safe.
  2. Touch Rest (heart) → IC Rest.
  3. Wait for "You are fully recovered!", or tap Stop Resting.
  4. Between scenes (OOC), use OOC Reset instead.

docs.title: Passive stamina drain (optional)
docs.text:
Some universes enable a slow stamina drain — for example −1 stamina every 10 minutes in roleplay or tournament, with optional extra drain while moving. It's off by default, and there's no drain in OOC, AFK, or ★ modes. Ask staff whether your region uses it.

In-world rest objects (beds, campfires) may also restore vitals on their own timers — sit and follow their instructions.

docs.title: The Players HUD
docs.text: The Players HUD is the attachment you wear to play Feudalism 4. It tracks your health, stamina, mana, and XP, runs combat, talks to weapons, armor, and world objects, and opens the Setup HUD when you need to change your character.

docs.title: Two layers
docs.text: Layer What you see How to open it Players HUD (in-world prims) Glass spheres for Health / Stamina / Mana, an XP bar, and action buttons Always visible while worn Setup HUD (web panel) A full character editor in a browser panel Touch Options, or it opens on first use

docs.title: Displays
docs.text: Display Meaning Health sphere Your current health (damage from combat and hazards). Stamina sphere Your current stamina (exertion). Mana sphere Your current mana — hidden or empty if your character has no mana. Number prims The exact current value beside each sphere. XP bar Visual progress of your total XP toward the next milestone. XP text Your total XP (for example, 3,450 XP).

docs.title: Buttons
docs.text:
The exact labels vary slightly by build, but these are the common controls:

Button What it does Weapon slots (primary / secondary) Draw or sheath your equipped weapons. See Weapons & Armor. Action / tool slots (9 of them) Quick-use your worn tools — pouch, bucket, skin, buffs. See Action Slots. Options Opens or toggles the Setup HUD. Sync / Update Saves your XP and current pools, and refreshes your character data. Touch this after big XP gains. Rest (heart) Opens the rest menu — IC Rest, OOC Reset, Stop Resting. See Modes & Resting. Inventory Opens your character's personal inventory menu (Consume, Drop, To Pouch). See Personal Inventory. Coins Opens the currency menu, if your universe uses coin. See Currency & Coins.

Only you can use your own HUD. Touch the labeled prim to activate a control.

Player steps — a normal session

  1. Attach the HUD (not rezzed) and accept the Experience permission.
  2. First time? A starter character is usually created automatically in the default universe — you can play immediately and open Options later to customize.
  3. Play: watch the spheres and chat for +N XP messages.
  4. Touch Options to edit stats, class, inventory, or mode.
  5. Touch Sync/Update after heavy XP gain or before spending points.
  6. Touch Rest → IC Rest to recover; Stop Resting before travel or combat.

docs.title: The Sync/Update button matters
docs.text:
Your XP and vitals are held by the HUD during play and saved to fast game storage automatically a few seconds after each change. The Sync/Update button forces an immediate save and pulls the latest data from the cloud. Use it:

  • after earning a lot of XP,
  • right before opening the Setup HUD to spend points,
  • after changing regions, if your stats look stale.

No Experience permission = no saving

If you didn't accept the Experience popup, your XP and pools won't persist. Detach and re-wear the HUD to get the prompt again.

See also: The Setup HUD · Stats & Vitals · XP & Available Points

docs.title: The Setup HUD
docs.text:
The Setup HUD is a web panel that appears on your HUD when you touch Options. It's where you create and edit your character.

On first wear, if you have no character yet, the panel may open briefly while the game auto-creates a starter character (see First-Time Setup), then returns you to play. Open Options anytime to customize that character or create another.

Changes you save here sync to the same character record the Players HUD uses in play.

docs.title: Tabs
docs.text: Tab What it's for Character Name, title, gender, species, universe, and your character picker + summary. Stats All 20 attributes, your Available Points, and + / − buttons (with class caps shown as /max). Classes Your current class and the class gallery, with each class's stat caps. Inventory Redirect — open inventory with the HUD I button in-world. Currency: HUD $ button when enabled. Options Game mode and show/hide options for your floating bars. Help In-panel instructions. Admin Staff-only tools — hidden unless you have admin access.

docs.title: Creating a character
docs.text:
Automatic: With no existing character in the default universe, the HUD provisions a starter (human, baseline stats, your display name or "Traveler") so you can play immediately. Customize later via the tabs below.

Manual: Use the Character tab's numbered step bar when you want full control from the start — see First-Time Setup for the full walkthrough.

docs.title: Editing an existing character
docs.text:
Player steps — edit

  1. Touch Options on the HUD to open the Setup HUD.
  2. On the Character tab, pick your character from the dropdown.
  3. Edit name, title, stats, class, etc.
  4. Click Save Character (it warns you if there are unsaved changes).
  5. On the Players HUD, touch Sync/Update to push your pools and XP to game storage.

docs.title: Switching characters
docs.text:
If you have multiple characters in a universe, select another from the Character dropdown and load it. Your personal inventory follows the character — see Personal Inventory & Pouches for how that works.

Spend points here, earn XP in play

You earn XP by playing (the Players HUD tracks it), and you spend it on the Setup HUD's Stats tab. Touch Sync/Update before opening Setup so your latest XP is reflected as Available Points.

docs.title: Species, Classes & Genders
docs.text: When you create a character you choose a gender, a species, and a class. Together they shape your starting stats, your minimums and maximums, and whether you can use mana.

docs.title: Genders
docs.text: Six options: Male, Female, Transgender, Hermaphrodite, Non-Binary, Other. Pick a portrait, then confirm.

docs.title: Species
docs.text:
There are many species in the base game — examples include Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Gnome, Dragonborn, Half-Elf, Half-Orc, Tiefling, Drow, Demon, Imp, Werewolf, Vampire, Merfolk, Fairy, Satyr, Minotaur, Reptilian, Goblin — and your universe may add its own.

Each species defines:

  • a default stat line (several attributes start above the base of 2);
  • stat minimums you can't go below when lowering a stat;
  • stat maximums (racial caps on certain attributes);
  • a mana chance at creation — whether your character can cast at all.

Humans are the beginner-friendly pick

Humans start at default stats plus 10 Available Points, letting you customize freely. Other species trade that flexibility for stronger or more specialized starting lines.

docs.title: Classes
docs.text:
Classes are your character's vocation — Knight, Archer, Mage, Thief, Farmer, Bard, Assassin, Paladin, Peasant, Smith, Ranger, and many more.

Each class sets a maximum for each stat, shown as /max next to the stat on the Setup HUD's Stats tab. You can't raise a stat above:

  • your class cap for that stat, or
  • the absolute maximum of 9.

So your class shapes where you can specialize, while your species shapes where you start.

docs.title: Putting it together
docs.text:
graph TD
  A[Species] -->|sets defaults, mins, maxes, mana| C[Your starting character]
  B[Class] -->|sets per-stat caps| C
  D[Available Points] -->|spent to raise stats| C

Your universe may filter the list

Admins can limit which species, classes, and genders are offered in their universe — so your in-world menu may be shorter than the full list above. See Universes, Species & Classes.

Next: spend your points wisely with XP & Available Points.

docs.title: Stats & Vitals
docs.text: Your character is defined by 20 attributes and three vitals (health, stamina, mana). Attributes are the dice the game rolls for you; vitals are the pools that keep you standing.

docs.title: The 20 attributes
docs.text:
Agility · Animal Handling · Athletics · Awareness · Crafting · Deception · Endurance · Entertaining · Fighting · Healing · Influence · Intelligence · Knowledge · Marksmanship · Persuasion · Stealth · Survival · Thievery · Will · Wisdom

Every attribute starts at 2 before your species and class adjust it. Higher attributes mean more dice when the game rolls for an action, so you succeed more often and more strongly.

docs.title: Vitals — how they're calculated
docs.text:
Vital Formula Default factor Health (max) (Agility + Athletics) × factor 25 Stamina (max) (Endurance + Will) × factor 25 Mana (max) (Wisdom + Intelligence) × factor 25 — only if your character has mana

At all-2 attributes with the default factor, that's 100 / 100 / 100. Raising the underlying attributes raises the matching pool.

No mana? That's normal

Whether a character has mana is decided at creation (by species roll and universe settings). If your mana bar stays at 0, your character simply isn't a mana user — see the Mana tab below.

docs.title: What each vital means in play
docs.text:
HealthStaminaMana

Damage from melee, arrows, hazards, and some world objects reduces health. At 0 you pass out ("You pass out from your wounds!"). Recover by resting, eating, healing magic, or a healer character.

Represents exertion. Some universes enable a slow passive drain (and extra drain while moving); it's off by default. Hitting 0 stamina can also knock you out. Recover by resting.

The arcane resource for mana-using characters. It recovers while resting if you have mana. If you can't cast and have no mana bar, your character isn't a caster.

docs.title: Which stats matter for what
docs.text: Activity Main attributes Melee combat Fighting (attack & defense), Agility (dodge) Archery / ranged Marksmanship (vs the target's Athletics when hitting a player) Crafting stations Crafting Harvesting / farming Crafting, Survival Stealth & theft Stealth, Thievery Social roleplay Persuasion, Influence, Deception, Entertaining Healing Healing, Wisdom

docs.title: Checking your pools
docs.text:
• Read the three spheres and the number prims on the Players HUD.
  • Or wear the Meter for floating bars (enable via Setup HUD → Options → Show Bars).
  • After you change stats in the Setup HUD, your pools recalculate from the new attributes.

To raise attributes, you spend Available Points — see XP & Available Points.

docs.title: XP & Available Points
docs.text: You earn XP by playing and spend Available Points to raise your stats. This page explains how they relate, how XP is saved, and exactly what raising a stat costs.

docs.title: The key numbers
docs.text: Term Meaning XP (Experience) Your running total, earned by playing. Shown on the HUD and in chat as +N XP. Available Points What you spend on the Stats tab to raise attributes. Conversion 1,000 XP = 1 Available Point. Partial thousands don't count until you hit the next full 1,000.

docs.title: How you gain XP
docs.text:
Wear the attached HUD on an Experience-enabled region and accept permissions. Typical awards:

Activity Typical XP Melee hit +1 per successful hit Knockout — you lose +5 Knockout — you win Scaled by the opponent's toughness Crafting success (Challenge Rating + 1) × 10 Crafting failure Challenge Rating + 1 Harvest success CR × 2 (failure: CR) Archery target (RP mode) Per arrow; more for hits, inner rings, and distance Practice-dummy bow kill 3 base, +1 over 100 m, +1 over 200 m Practice-dummy melee kill Usually equals the dummy's CR

You'll see a confirmation in local chat like [Feudalism 4] +25 XP.

Challenge Rating (CR)

Most crafting and harvesting objects have a Challenge Rating — a difficulty number set by the owner. Higher CR means harder rolls but more XP. You can read more in How Crafting Works.

docs.title: How Available Points are calculated
docs.text:
Available Points =
      species default budget
    + human bonus (+10 if you are Human)
    + floor(total XP / 1000)
    − points already spent on your current stats
Total XP Points from XP alone 0 – 999 0 1,000 – 1,999 1 5,500 5 12,000 12
  • Humans start with +10 points at racial defaults.
  • Other species start at 0 at defaults, but lowering a stat below its starting value refunds points you can spend elsewhere.

docs.title: What raising a stat costs
docs.text:
Costs are exponential — each step up is twice the last:

Raise Cost 1 → 2 1 2 → 3 2 3 → 4 4 4 → 5 8 5 → 6 16 6 → 7 32 7 → 8 64 8 → 9 128
  • Lowering a stat refunds the cost of the step you remove (but never below your species minimum).
  • Limits: you can't exceed your class cap for a stat, or the absolute maximum of 9.

docs.title: How XP is saved and loaded
docs.text:
• During play, the HUD holds your XP and flushes it to fast game storage automatically about 8 seconds after your last gain.
  • Manual saves happen when you: detach the HUD, touch Sync/Update, open the Setup HUD, stop resting, or leave roleplay mode.
  • Loads happen when you: attach the HUD, enter roleplay mode, switch characters, or load in the Setup HUD.

Accept the Experience permission

Without it, your XP and pools won't persist. Re-wear the HUD and accept the popup.

docs.title: Spending points — step by step
docs.text:
Player steps

  1. Earn XP in play (watch for +N XP).
  2. Touch Sync/Update on the Players HUD if you just earned a lot.
  3. Touch Options → Stats tab.
  4. Check Available Points at the top.
  5. Use − / + on each stat (the tooltip shows the cost).
  6. Click Save Character, then return to roleplay.

The XP bar is just a visual

The HUD's XP texture bar fills toward milestones at 1,000 / 5,000 / 10,000 / 100,000 / 1,000,000 XP. That's a different scale from the 1,000 XP = 1 point rule — don't confuse the two.

docs.title: Combat
docs.text:
Feudalism combat is a hybrid: you physically aim and position your character, but dice driven by your stats decide hits and damage. This section covers melee, archery, your gear, and where to train.

  • Melee Combat — drawing weapons, strike ranges, and how a hit is resolved.
  • Archery — bows, valid arrows, targets, and managed ranges.
  • Weapons & Armor — slots, weapon stats, and armor protection values.
  • Training Grounds — practice dummies and archery ranges to grind skill and XP.

Consent and rules are local

The mechanics here describe how fights resolve. Whether you can fight, capture, or kill another player is governed by your universe's consent and death rules. Always follow local policy.

docs.title: Archery
docs.text: Archery combines physical aiming — your arrow must actually collide with the target — with a Marksmanship dice check. It works with common Second Life bows.

docs.title: What you need
docs.text:
• The Feudalism 4 Players HUD (for Marksmanship and XP).
  • A bow and valid arrows: the rezzed arrow's object name must contain arrow. Names containing barrow are ignored.

Closer is easier

Difficulty scales with distance. A target that's trivial at 15 m can be a real challenge at 40 m.

docs.title: Shooting a practice target
docs.text:
Player steps

  1. Wear your F4 HUD.
  2. Stand at the firing line and shoot the face of the target.
  3. Read your own local chat (results are private to you, not sim-wide).

Example messages:

  • You miss the Practice Butt (32 m).
  • You hit the Practice Butt: blue ring — 3 points (32 m).
  • You hit the Practice Butt: bullseye (yellow) — 5 points (18 m).

Scoring: your Marksmanship is checked against the target's Challenge Rating, harder at longer range. Beat it and you score a ring (outer rings = fewer points). Roleplay targets grant XP; arcade targets only score points.

docs.title: Managed archery ranges
docs.text:
Larger ranges control which targets you may shoot, and use official lane distances instead of where you happen to be standing.

Player steps — managed range

  1. Wear your F4 HUD.
  2. Go to your lane (1, 2, 3, …).
  3. Stand on the lane's firing pad until chat confirms, e.g. "Lane 2 — shoot the targets ahead. Official distances apply."
  4. Shoot only your lane's targets.
  5. Stay on the pad while firing — distance uses the range's official figure.
  6. Step off when finished.

If you shoot a range-controlled target without registering, you'll see: "This target is range-controlled. Stand on the lane firing pad to shoot."

docs.title: Hitting a player with an arrow
docs.text:
When an arrow physically strikes another player's HUD body:

  • Base damage is 5.
  • It's adjusted by a contest of the shooter's Marksmanship vs the target's Athletics.
  • A confirmed hit always deals at least 1 damage.
  • A knockout awards XP as usual.

docs.title: Archery XP
docs.text: Activity XP Scored arrow on a roleplay target Always some; more for hits, inner rings, and distance Practice-dummy bow kill 3 base, +1 over 100 m, +1 over 200 m

docs.title: Training tips
docs.text:
• Raise Marksmanship with Available Points and steady practice.
  • Some longbow-style arrow names add a small bonus on certain targets.
  • On managed ranges, one archer per firing pad.

See Training Grounds for dummies and ranges.

docs.title: Melee Combat
docs.text: Melee in Feudalism 4 is resolved by dice and stats, not by your sword mesh physically touching the target. When you attack, your weapon broadcasts the strike and the defender's HUD rolls to defend.

docs.title: Player steps
docs.text:
How to fight in melee

  1. Wear your Feudalism 4 Players HUD.
  2. Attach a Feudalism melee weapon (primary and/or secondary).
  3. Touch the primary (or secondary) weapon slot on the HUD to draw it.
  4. Stand within the weapon's strike range (varies by weapon).
  5. Use the weapon as its instructions say — usually click to attack.
  6. Read local chat for hit/miss, body location, damage, and remaining health.
  7. Sheath when you're done (tap the weapon slot again).

docs.title: How a hit is resolved
docs.text:
1. Attack roll — the attacker rolls dice equal to their Fighting, plus a random weapon bonus.
  2. Defense roll — the defender rolls Fighting dice plus a random Agility dodge bonus.

  3. Modifiers adjust the rolls:

    Situation Effect Defender moving fast (> 3.2 m/s) Defender +5 dodge; attacker −5 attack Attacker on higher ground Attacker +1 attack Defender on higher ground Defender +1 dodge Impairment (drunk, etc.) −1 dodge per level Tournament mode Combat stats normalized to 5 for both sides

  4. If attack > defense, it's a hit.

  5. Damage uses the weapon's base damage (a random roll from 1 to 2× the weapon's damage), reduced by any armor on the randomly chosen body part, with a bonus for a strong margin of success.

docs.title: Body parts
docs.text: Hits land on one of 9 locations: head, neck, upper torso, lower torso, right arm, left arm, upper leg, lower leg, foot. Armor protects per location, so coverage matters.

docs.title: Dropped weapons
docs.text: On an extreme roll (a margin of 6+ degrees of success or failure), there's a 30% chance you fumble and drop your weapon. Pick it back up and re-draw.

docs.title: Knockout & recovery
docs.text: When your health or stamina hits 0, you pass out ("You pass out from your wounds!"). Recover with IC Rest, healing magic, or a healer — see Game Modes & Resting.

docs.title: XP from melee
docs.text:
• +1 per successful hit.
  • A knockout awards more: the loser gets +5, and the winner's award scales with the opponent's toughness.

Armor reduction is still maturing

Some current builds may not fully apply melee armor reduction. If your universe is mid-transition, ask staff which combat features are live locally.

See also: Weapons & Armor · Training Grounds

docs.title: Training Grounds
docs.text: Practice objects let you grind combat skill and XP safely, without another player.

docs.title: Practice dummies
docs.text:
A practice dummy has floating HP and accepts both melee and bow attacks. Kill it and it respawns after a reset delay.

Bow trainingMelee training

Player steps

  1. Wear your F4 HUD.
  2. Shoot the dummy with valid arrows (name contains arrow).
  3. Chat reports glance/hit, damage, and HP remaining.
  4. A kill awards XP (3 base for a bow kill, plus distance bonuses).

Player steps

  1. Wear your F4 HUD and attach a Feudalism weapon.
  2. Draw it from the HUD weapon slot.
  3. Attack within the weapon's range.
  4. Chat reports the hit or fail, damage, and HP. A kill awards XP roughly equal to the dummy's CR.

A dummy is configured by its owner with a name, HP, reset delay, and challenge rating — for example a 100-HP dummy that respawns after 60 seconds.

docs.title: Archery targets & ranges
docs.text:
• Practice targets (butts) score rings and grant XP in roleplay mode. See Archery.
  • Managed ranges assign you a lane and use official distances; stand on the firing pad to shoot.

docs.title: Making the most of training
docs.text:
• Train the stat you care about: Fighting for melee, Marksmanship for bows.
  • Vary your distance on ranges — longer shots are worth more XP but are harder.
  • Convert XP to stat gains on the Stats tab between sessions.

Etiquette on shared ranges

One archer per firing pad, and shoot only your assigned lane's targets. It keeps everyone's scores clean.

docs.title: Weapons & Armor
docs.text: Your HUD has dedicated weapon slots and auto-detects armor you wear, feeding both into combat.

docs.title: Weapon slots
docs.text:
Slot Use Primary Your main weapon — draw/sheath from the HUD. Secondary A sidearm or shield — draw/sheath from the HUD.

Touch a slot to draw the weapon; touch again to sheath. You must have a weapon drawn to attack (including against practice dummies and huntable animals).

docs.title: Weapon stats
docs.text:
Feudalism weapons carry stats that affect combat:

  • Damage — base damage; a hit rolls a random 1 to 2× this value.
  • Speed — how quickly you can attack.
  • Weight — heavier weapons can be more demanding.
  • Min / max range — you must be within this band to land a strike (a longsword, for example, sits around ~0.65–1.6 m).

There are 16 weapon types in the system (dagger, sword, mace, and so on). Each behaves a little differently — read the weapon's own instructions.

docs.title: Armor
docs.text:
Wear Feudalism armor attachments and they auto-register with your HUD. Armor protects on a 0–8 scale, applied to the body part that gets hit:

Armor Protection None 0 Cloth 1 Fur 2 Leather 3 Chainmail 4 Ring mail 5 Scale mail 6 Brigandine 7 Plate 8

A shield is handled separately and can add to your defense when paired with a weapon in roleplay.

Coverage matters

Because hits land on one of 9 body parts at random, covering more locations with armor is better than a single heavily-armored piece. Note that some current builds may not fully apply melee armor reduction — confirm with your universe.

See Melee Combat for how these values feed into a hit.

docs.title: Crafting & Professions
docs.text:
Crafting turns raw materials into food, drink, tools, weapons, and trade goods. Most stations share the same flow — learn it once and you can use them all.

  • How Crafting Works — the universal check → consume → produce loop.
  • Cooking — ovens, cauldrons, and food plates.
  • Smithing & Metalwork — workstations, smith tables, sharpening, and repair.
  • Leatherworking — turning hides and skins into leather goods.
  • Brewing & Winemaking — presses, fermenters, and producers.
  • Alchemy & Herbs — herb pots and reagent processing.
  • Milling & Grain — mills and silos for flour and meal.

Two audiences

These pages explain how to use crafting as a player. If you own stations and want to add or edit recipes, head to Using Recipes and the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: Alchemy & Herbs
docs.text: Alchemy and herbalism cover growing herbs in pots and processing reagents into distillates, powders, and other ingredients.

docs.title: Herb pots
docs.text:
An herb pot works just like a small farm field, but in a planter. It grows up to 9 herb plants.

Player steps

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch (plus a water source if the herb needs water).
  2. Touch the pot and confirm planting — seeds (and water/stover) are taken from your inventory.
  3. Wait the grow time (~CR × 100 seconds).
  4. Touch each grown plant to harvest the herb.

The harvest yields the specific herb the pot is configured for (for example, parsley).

docs.title: Processing reagents
docs.text:
Refining raw reagents into usable alchemical ingredients runs through a workstation with ordinary crafting recipes — pick the recipe, it consumes inputs, rolls your Crafting, and produces the output. Typical results include distillates, crushed powders/dusts, and refined reagents, often made from a raw drop plus something like water or salt.

graph LR
  Herb[Herbs / raw reagents] --> Pot[Herb pot grows herbs]
  Herb --> Bench[Workstation: refine]
  Bench --> R[Distillates / powders / refined reagents]

docs.title: Player steps — refine a reagent
docs.text:
How to refine

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch with the raw reagents.
  2. Touch the workstation; pick the alchemy/reagent recipe.
  3. Confirm; wait the timer; your Crafting is rolled against the recipe's CR.
  4. The refined product (and sometimes a bonus quantity) goes into your pouch.

What alchemy does is set by your universe

The base system handles producing reagents and ingredients. Whether a finished potion grants a buff, healing, or another effect depends on your universe's consumable and effect setup. Ask staff what your region's potions do.

Owners: see the Recipe Notecard Reference for herb-pot config lines and workstation recipes.

docs.title: Brewing & Winemaking
docs.text: Drinks are made in stages: a producer station turns a raw input into a base liquid, an oven / fermenter turns that into the finished drink, and a refillable container can store or serve it.

docs.title: Liquids vs solids
docs.text: Liquids (water, must, mash, wine, ale) are stored in your HUD personal inventory like any other item. Wear a drink skin and water bucket so stations can route liquid give/take correctly; solids (grapes, barley, honey, yeast) live in your pouch or personal inventory. (In recipe data, liquid names carry a trailing *, but you never see that in menus.)

docs.title: Producer stations (no roll)
docs.text:
Presses, malting tubs, grape-stomping vats, and churns are producers: one input → one output, on a timer, with no success roll. If you have the input and wait, you get the output.

Player steps — producer

  1. Wear your pouch (and skin if a liquid is involved).
  2. Touch the station (or sit for animated ones like a stomping vat).
  3. Pick the recipe and confirm.
  4. Wait the timer; the output goes to your personal inventory (or pouch overflow).

Examples: a grape press turns grapes → must; a malting tub turns barley → malt; a churn turns milk → cream → butter.

docs.title: The winemaking chain
docs.text:
graph LR
  G[Grapes - pouch] --> P[Press or stomp vat]
  P --> M[Must - skin]
  M --> F[Fermenter oven]
  F --> W[Wine - pouch]
  W --> B[Barrel - store/serve]
  1. Gather grapes.
  2. Press or stomp them into must.
  3. Ferment the must (with yeast, water, and sometimes sugar) at an oven into wine.
  4. Optionally store wine in a refillable barrel for serving.

docs.title: The brewing chain
docs.text:
The same idea makes ale, beer, spirits, and mead:

  • Malt barley → mash it (with water) → ferment with hops + yeast into ale/beer.
  • Distill mash with sugar into spirits/whiskey.
  • Ferment honey + yeast + water into mead.

docs.title: Refillable containers
docs.text:
Barrels, kegs, and milk pails are refillable containers: touch them to view Contents, Add Item, or Take Item. Liquids use your drink skin. Linked production (like milking) can refill them automatically.

Skins and buckets are filters, not tanks

On Feudalism 4 personal-inventory HUDs, liquids live in your character inventory. Touch your drink skin (or action slot 8) for brewing liquids; use your bucket (slot 9) or the water filter for water. Wells fill water into personal inventory while you wear a bucket.

Owners: producer and fermenter recipe formats are in the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: Cooking
docs.text: Cooking is done at ovens and cauldrons (multi-ingredient recipes with a roll), while food plates serve ready-made meals at a table.

docs.title: Ovens & cauldrons
docs.text:
The oven is the workhorse cooking, brewing, and fermenting station. It follows the universal crafting loop.

Player steps

  1. Wear your pouch (and a drink skin if the recipe uses liquids like water).
  2. Touch the oven — or sit on it if it's set up that way.
  3. If the oven requires a fire, make sure it's lit first.
  4. Pick a category, then a recipe, then confirm Proceed?
  5. Ingredients are removed; wait (CR + 1) × 20 seconds.
  6. Your Crafting is rolled against the recipe's CR — beat it to succeed.

XP: success (CR + 1) × 10, failure CR + 1.

Ovens make a huge range of things depending on their recipe notecard — soups, breads, roasts, and the base liquids for brewing and winemaking.

Physical plates and props

Recipes can hand you a physical object (e.g. a food plate). If the oven doesn't hold that object, your HUD delivers it from the estate DropBox after a successful craft. Wear your HUD and accept Experience permissions. Owners: DropBox & physical delivery.

docs.title: Food plates
docs.text:
A food plate serves shared, ready-made food at a table. It does not come from your pouch — each plate has a limited number of servings that heal or drain your vitals through the HUD.

Player steps

  1. Wear your F4 HUD (no pouch needed).
  2. Touch the plate within 5 m and choose Eat.
  3. A serving is consumed and your health/stamina adjust accordingly.

A plate is configured with a display name, the health and stamina it grants, and how many servings it holds.

docs.title: Eating from your pouch
docs.text:
Separately, you can Eat edible items directly from your pouch — useful for trail food. This is different from a shared food plate.

Plan around fire and seating

If an oven won't open, check whether it needs you to sit or needs a lit fire — both are common owner settings. The oven usually tells you.

Owners: cooking recipe format is in the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: How Crafting Works
docs.text: Almost every crafting station in Feudalism 4 follows the same rhythm. Learn this once and ovens, smith tables, fields, mills, and producers all make sense.

docs.title: The universal loop
docs.text:
graph LR
  A[Touch or sit] --> B[Pick a recipe]
  B --> C[Station checks your ingredients]
  C --> D{Have them all?}
  D -- No --> X[It tells you what's missing]
  D -- Yes --> E[Confirm: Proceed?]
  E --> F[Ingredients consumed]
  F --> G[Timer runs]
  G --> H[Crafting roll vs CR]
  H -- Success --> I[You receive the output]
  H -- Failure --> J[Ingredients gone, partial XP]
  1. Open the station — touch it (some require you to sit, and a few need a lit fire nearby).
  2. Pick a recipe from the category → recipe menus.
  3. Ingredient check — the station searches your inventory (and worn containers) and tells you if anything's missing.
  4. Confirm — when you proceed, the ingredients are removed immediately.
  5. Timer — production takes time, usually (CR + 1) × 20 seconds.
  6. Crafting roll — your Crafting is rolled against the recipe's Challenge Rating (CR). Beat it and you succeed.
  7. Output — success gives you the finished item; failure means the ingredients are spent for only a small XP consolation.

docs.title: Challenge Rating (CR)
docs.text:
Every recipe has a CR — its difficulty. CR drives three things:

CR affects How Time Higher CR → longer craft (about (CR+1)×20 s on ovens/smiths). Difficulty Your Crafting roll must beat the CR roll. XP Success ≈ (CR + 1) × 10; failure ≈ CR + 1.

If your HUD doesn't report a Crafting value, stations assume a default of 4.

docs.title: Ingredients & where they come from
docs.text:
Stations pull ingredients from the right place automatically:

Ingredient type Taken from Solids (flour, carrot, iron ore, yeast, …) HUD personal inventory and worn pouches water Personal inventory — worn bucket at wells; slot 9 or water filter Other liquids (must, wine, …) Personal inventory — worn drink skin; slot 8 or liquids filter

You never choose which container — the system finds and consumes what it needs across everything you're carrying. See Personal Inventory & Pouches.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Depending on the recipe, your reward is either:

  • a virtual item added to your inventory (most food, materials, and goods), or
  • a liquid added to your drink skin or bucket, or
  • a physical object handed to you — from the station's own inventory, or from the estate DropBox if the station doesn't stock that object (requires your HUD and Experience permissions).

See DropBox & physical delivery (owners) for central inventory setup.

docs.title: Stations that skip the roll
docs.text:
Some simple producer stations (presses, churns, malting tubs) have no success roll — if you have the ingredients and wait the timer, you always get the output. See Brewing & Winemaking.

Raise Crafting to waste fewer materials

Because failure still consumes ingredients, a higher Crafting stat directly saves you resources on risky, high-CR recipes. Spend points on Crafting if you craft a lot — see XP & Available Points.

docs.title: Leatherworking
docs.text: Leatherworking turns animal hides and skins into leather and leather goods. In the base system this runs through a smith workstation using ordinary crafting recipes.

docs.title: The basic chain
docs.text:
graph LR
  H[Hunt / livestock] --> S[Raw hide or skin]
  S --> W[Workstation recipe]
  W --> L[Leather]
  L --> G[Leather goods / armor materials]
  1. Obtain a raw hide or skin from hunting or livestock.
  2. At a workstation, pick the tanning recipe; it consumes the hide (often plus something like salt).
  3. Run the timer and Crafting roll to produce leather.
  4. Use the leather in further recipes for armor materials or goods.

For example, a workstation recipe might turn a special skin plus salt into a finished leather at a modest Challenge Rating.

docs.title: Player steps
docs.text:
How to tan

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch, carrying the hide/skin and any extra ingredients.
  2. Touch the workstation and pick the leather recipe.
  3. Confirm; wait the timer; your Crafting is rolled against the recipe's CR.
  4. The finished leather goes into your pouch.

Leather as armor

Leather armor protects at value 3 on the armor scale. The crafted material feeds into whatever leather armor your universe offers.

Owners: define tanning recipes like any other workstation recipe — see the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: Milling & Grain
docs.text: Mills and silos process bulk grain into flour, meal, and dried products — the bridge between farming and cooking.

docs.title: Grain mill
docs.text:
A grain mill grinds grain into flour and meal in batches.

Player steps

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch with the grain to mill.
  2. Touch the mill within range and pick a recipe (e.g. flour from wheat berry, meal from corn).
  3. Choose how many batches to run.
  4. Wait the processing time (~CR × 100 seconds), with a short cooldown (~60 s) afterward.
  5. Each successful batch rolls your Crafting; the output goes to your pouch.

Example outputs: corn meal, flour, rye flour, and finer grades like fine flour at higher CR.

docs.title: Grain silo
docs.text: A grain silo dries or processes bulk grain into a stored product. The flow mirrors the mill: touch, pick a recipe and a batch count, wait ~CR × 100 s, and collect the output to your pouch.

docs.title: A grain-to-bread chain
docs.text:
graph LR
  Field[Field: wheat] --> Berry[Wheat berry]
  Berry --> Mill[Mill: flour]
  Mill --> Oven[Oven: bread]

Batch wisely

Milling many batches at once is efficient, but each batch still rolls Crafting — a higher Crafting stat means more of your grain survives to become flour.

Owners: mill and silo recipes use the config notecard format in the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: Smithing & Metalwork
docs.text: Metalwork covers turning raw materials into refined goods, tools, and weapons at a smith workstation, plus maintaining your gear with sharpening and repair.

docs.title: The smith workstation
docs.text:
A smith workstation is a general fabrication station that follows the universal crafting loop: pick a recipe, it checks and consumes your materials, runs a timer, rolls your Crafting, and gives you the output.

Player steps

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch.
  2. Touch the workstation (or sit, if configured) within range.
  3. Pick a category → recipe → confirm.
  4. Wait (CR + 1) × 20 seconds; your Crafting is rolled vs the recipe's CR.
  5. On success, the finished item lands in your pouch.

Smith recipes can produce processed minerals, refined reagents, crafting materials, and more — for example turning raw ore or special drops into refined goods. Multi-output workstations can grant several items from one recipe (pouch goods plus physical trophies, hides, etc.).

Physical outputs & DropBox

If a recipe produces a physical object the station doesn't keep in inventory, your HUD delivers it from the estate DropBox after success. Same rules as ovens — see DropBox & physical delivery.

One smith at a time

A workstation locks to a single user until the craft finishes (with a timeout safety net), so you won't collide with another crafter mid-job.

docs.title: Sharpening your weapons
docs.text:
A sharpening wheel keeps an edged weapon in good condition.

Player steps

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch, and draw your primary weapon.
  2. Touch the wheel within 5 m.
  3. If the weapon's condition is 20–100, you can sharpen; below 20 it's too damaged (use a smith/anvil).
  4. Confirm — after about 30 seconds, your Crafting is rolled against CR 2.
  5. Success raises the weapon's condition toward 100 (+2 XP); failure gives 1 XP and no improvement.

docs.title: Heavy repair (anvil)
docs.text: Badly damaged weapons need an anvil, which uses a sit-based hammer/grind process and then sends you to a whetstone for finishing. Anvils are a Feudalism 3 station using the Experience system; on F4 regions the routine maintenance path is the sharpening wheel. Confirm with staff what's available.

docs.title: Leather, wool, and other materials
docs.text:
General workstations also craft non-metal materials (for example, processing a hide into leather). See Leatherworking.

Smelting note

There's no separate smelter object in the base system — ore → ingot is typically a smith/workstation recipe (or a universe's custom station). See Mining.

Owners: smith recipe formats (including multi-output) are in the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: Living off the Land
docs.text:
Raw materials are the start of every crafting chain. Here's how to gather them — by hand, with a pickaxe, with a bow, or by tending crops and animals.

  • Gathering & Foraging — bushes, herbs, fruit, wood, and generic nodes.
  • Mining — ore and stone from nodes and multi-deposit mines.
  • Hunting — wild animals, loot, and kill XP.
  • Farming — fields, crops, compost, silos, and mills.
  • Livestock, Wool & Milk — renewable harvests from animals.
  • Beekeeping & Honey — colonize a hive and harvest honey.
  • Mulberry Silkery — closed silkworm colony; harvest silk cocoons.

The universal rule: wear your HUD (and pouches for extra capacity)

Almost everything you gather goes into your personal inventory on the HUD, overflowing to worn pouches when needed. Wear at least one pouch for crafting checks and bulk storage — up to two on current Feudalism 4 builds — before you start. Many objects also have a range (commonly 5–15 m) and a security setting that decides who's allowed to use them.

docs.title: Beekeeping & Honey
docs.text: A beehive (Honey Maker) is a slow, passive producer. You establish a colony, then harvest honey as it fills over real time.

docs.title: Player steps
docs.text:
How to keep bees

  1. Touch the hive and choose Colonize!
  2. Wait about 5 real minutes while the colony establishes.
  3. At the end, your Crafting is rolled to set up the queen and the forage quality.
  4. If the queen succeeds, honey fills passively up to the hive's capacity.
  5. Wear a pouch and touch the hive to Take 1, Take 5, or Take All.

docs.title: Forage quality & fill speed
docs.text:
The forage roll sets an efficiency tier based on where the hive is placed. Higher efficiency fills the hive faster:

Forage Approx. time to fill Low (~40%) ~10 hours Excellent (100%) ~4 hours

If you move the hive, touch it and choose Moved Hive to re-roll forage for the new spot (another ~5 minute wait). Reset clears the colony so you can start over.

docs.title: Reading the hive
docs.text:
The hive's floating text shows the current honey amount, forage quality, and efficiency. "Empty" just means production is still catching up — check back later.

Colonization failed — no queen
  • Try Colonize! again; higher Crafting improves your odds.
  • If security allows, a player with higher Crafting can start colonization for the hive.

Version note

The Honey Maker originated in Feudalism 3 and uses the Experience-based Crafting roll. If your region is on Feudalism 4, confirm with staff that the hive is supported locally.

Honey feeds into Brewing & Winemaking (mead) and various cooking recipes.

docs.title: Farming
docs.text: Farming turns seeds and water into crops over time. The main objects are fields, compost bins, and grain processors (silos and mills).

docs.title: Farm fields
docs.text:
A field grows up to 9 plants. You plant, wait for a grow timer, then harvest each plant for a crop plus stover (plant waste useful for compost).

Player steps

  1. Wear your HUD and pouch (plus a water source if the crop needs water).
  2. Touch the field within 15 m when its floating text says it's ready.
  3. Confirm planting — the required seeds and water are taken from your inventory.
  4. Wait the grow time (roughly Challenge Rating × 100 seconds — about 5 minutes at CR 3).
  5. Each plant slot rolls Crafting vs the field's CR; successful slots sprout crop prims.
  6. Touch each grown plant to harvest a crop + stover.
  7. The field resets about a minute after the last harvest.

XP: each plant slot grants CR × 2 on success, CR on failure.

docs.title: Compost bins
docs.text:
Turn farm waste into fertilizer.

Player steps

  1. Wear your pouch; touch the bin within 5 m.
  2. Add Item — deposit accepted waste: stover, scraps, straw, manure, slop, or eggshells.
  3. It composts passively over time, or use Turn Compost to speed a batch.
  4. Take Item to withdraw the finished fertilizer.

docs.title: Grain silos & mills
docs.text:
These process bulk grain in batches:

  • Grain silo — dries bulk grain into a product. Touch, pick a recipe and batch count, wait (~CR × 100 s), and the output goes to your pouch.
  • Grain mill — grinds grain into flour and meal. See Milling & Grain.

docs.title: A typical farming chain
docs.text:
graph LR
  S[Seeds + Water] --> F[Field grows crop]
  F --> C[Crop + Stover]
  C --> M[Mill: flour/meal]
  C2[Stover] --> K[Compost: fertilizer]
  M --> Cook[Cooking recipes]

Owners: field, silo, and mill configuration is in Setting Up Stations and the Recipe Notecard Reference.

docs.title: Gathering & Foraging
docs.text: Gatherables are touch-to-collect nodes — bushes, herbs, fruit trees, wood, and generic resource spots. They drop items straight into your pouch.

docs.title: Player steps
docs.text:
How to forage

  1. Wear your pouch.
  2. Walk within 10 m and touch the node.
  3. Items arrive in your inventory (you'll see a chat message).
  4. Wait out the short cooldown before touching again.
  5. After enough harvests the node either resets (disappears, then returns later) or dies (gone for good).

docs.title: What to expect
docs.text:
Behavior Meaning Reset node Goes invisible when depleted, then reappears after its reset timer. Dying node Is removed permanently once exhausted — grab what you can. Cooldown A short wait (typically 1–30 seconds) between clicks. Security The owner decides who can gather: owner, group, or all.

Each node yields one or more items per click (set by its owner). A multi-yield bush can give several kinds at once (for example leaves and berries in fixed amounts every touch).

docs.title: Wood and other materials
docs.text:
There isn't a separate "woodcutting" object — logs and timber are configured as ordinary gatherables (for example, a tree node that yields log). So chopping wood works exactly like foraging a bush: touch it and collect. Your universe decides what each node yields.

You can't gather coin

gold coin, silver coin, and copper coin are blocked from gatherables on purpose. Currency only comes from the coin system.

Owners: see how to configure nodes in Setting Up Stations.

docs.title: Hunting
docs.text: Wild animals can be hunted with a bow or a melee weapon. When an animal dies it drops loot and awards XP, then respawns after a delay.

docs.title: What you need
docs.text:
• The F4 Players HUD (Marksmanship for bows, Fighting for melee, and to receive kill XP).
  • A bow with valid arrows (the arrow's name must contain arrow, not barrow), or a drawn Feudalism melee weapon.
  • A pouch to carry loot (you don't need one just to land the kill).

docs.title: Player steps
docs.text:
How to hunt

  1. Wear your HUD (and a pouch for loot).
  2. Approach the animal. Bow: shoot it with valid arrows. Melee: draw your weapon and strike in range.
  3. Read chat for hit/glance, damage, and the animal's remaining health.
  4. On death, loot rezzes on the ground and the animal hides, then respawns later.

docs.title: How the fight resolves
docs.text:
BowMelee

Your Marksmanship (plus any longbow-name bonus) is checked against the animal's difficulty, which rises with distance. A glance deals no damage.

Your Fighting dice are checked against the animal's melee challenge rating. Damage comes from your weapon's stats.

docs.title: Kill XP
docs.text:
Kill type XP Bow 3 base Bow, over 100 m +1 Bow, over 200 m +1 more (up to 5) Melee Equal to the animal's melee challenge rating

You must be wearing the F4 HUD to receive kill XP.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: The owner sets who may hunt an animal: owner, group, or all. If you're not allowed, you'll see "not allowed to hunt" and your attacks won't apply.

docs.title: Loot
docs.text:
Loot isn't a random table — every object in the animal's contents drops when it dies. What you get is exactly what the owner placed inside.

Some animals are also livestock

Certain creatures can be both hunted and harvested while alive (like shearing a sheep). See Livestock, Wool & Milk.

docs.title: Livestock, Wool & Milk
docs.text: Some animals give renewable harvests while alive — wool from sheep, milk from cows — instead of (or in addition to) being hunted.

docs.title: Renewable harvests
docs.text:
A renewable animal or plant slowly regrows a resource up to a maximum, and you harvest what's grown.

Player steps

  1. Wear your pouch.
  2. Touch the animal/object within range (commonly 8 m) when its text shows available stock.
  3. The grown resource (e.g. wool) transfers to your inventory.
  4. It regrows over time — come back later for more.

For example, a sheep might grow 1 wool every 2 minutes up to a stored maximum, with the owner choosing the verb ("shear"), the rate, and who's allowed.

docs.title: Animals that are both huntable and harvestable
docs.text:
Livestock animals can combine hunting (kill them for loot) with renewable harvest (shear/milk them while alive):

  • A short, authorized touch harvests the grown resource (wool, etc.).
  • A bow or melee attack treats the animal like a huntable animal for a kill and loot.

docs.title: Milk & dairy
docs.text:
Milking is usually a small object or animation that fills a milk pail (a refillable container). From there:

graph LR
  Cow[Milking] --> Pail[Milk pail: milk]
  Pail --> Churn1[Churn: milk to cream]
  Churn1 --> Churn2[Churn: cream to butter]

Take milk from the pail into your pouch, then use a churn (a simple producer station) to make cream and butter. See Brewing & Winemaking for how producer stations work — churns use the same one-input-to-one-output pattern.

Wool to cloth

Wool you shear is a raw material; turning it into yarn or cloth is handled by your universe's crafting recipes (often a workstation). Check what your region offers, and see Smithing & Metalwork for how general workstations craft materials.

docs.title: Mining
docs.text: Mining gets you ore and stone. There are two flavors: simple mining nodes and larger mines with several deposits.

docs.title: Mining nodes
docs.text:
A mining node works like a gatherable, but its texture changes between an active look and a depleted one (often plain rock) so you can see at a glance whether it's ready.

Player steps

  1. Wear your pouch.
  2. Touch the node within 10 m.
  3. Collect ore into your inventory; wait for the cooldown.
  4. When depleted, the node shows its "empty" texture and resets after a timer (or dies).

A node yields a specific ore (for example iron ore or copper ore), an amount per click, and a number of clicks before it depletes — all set by the owner.

docs.title: Mines (multiple deposits)
docs.text:
A mine is a single object holding several ore or stone deposits, organized into categories.

Player steps

  1. Wear your pouch and touch the mine within 15 m.
  2. Pick a category, then a specific deposit.
  3. You get one successful gather per touch session.
  4. Depleted deposits refill after their own reset timer.

docs.title: From ore to metal
docs.text:
Mining produces raw ore. Turning ore into usable metal (and then tools or weapons) happens at crafting stations — see Smithing & Metalwork.

Smelting

There isn't a dedicated standalone smelter object in the base system. Universes typically handle ore → ingot as a smith / workstation recipe (or a custom station). Check what your region provides.

Owners: deposit and node configuration lives in Setting Up Stations.

docs.title: Mulberry Silkery
docs.text: A silkery is a closed mulberry + silkworm colony — closer to a beehive than a gatherable bush. You attract moths, keep the bush alive with water and fertilizer, and harvest silk cocoons. Leaves and worms stay inside the object.

docs.title: Player steps
docs.text:
How to run a silkery

  1. Rez the silkery — it starts pre-watered and fertilized. Watch the meters; they drain over time.
  2. Keep water, fertilizer, and health up (health must stay around 60+).
  3. When the bush is healthy, Attract moth (about 5 real minutes). Moths skip a sick bush.
  4. After moths settle, eggs → worms → cocoons → moths continue while you top up supplies.
  5. Harvest cocoons as silk cocoon. Leave some so moths can renew.

docs.title: What the meters mean
docs.text:
Meter Meaning Water / Fert Player supplies. Both drain slowly while active. Leaves Grown by a healthy bush; eaten by worms (not taken by players). Health Bush condition. Hits 0 → colony dies. W / C / M / E Worms, cocoons, moths, eggs.

If the bush dies (neglect, overpopulation stress), the silkery goes Dormant for a while, then returns to Ready so you can Attract moth again.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Wear your HUD and pouch; water usually needs a bucket (slot 9). Fertilizer comes from compost.
  • Harvest most cocoons for silk, but not every last one if moths look low.
  • Security (owner / group / all) is set by the owner in the object Description.

Owners: see Setting Up Stations.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Welcome to Feudalism 4. This section gets you from "I just got a HUD" to "I'm playing" in a few minutes.

  • What is Feudalism 4?

    The overview — what the game is, the core loop, and how the pieces fit together.

  • First-Time Setup

    Wear the HUD, accept Experience permissions, and create your character.

  • Quick-Start Checklist

    A one-page checklist you can follow (or hand to a new player).

In a hurry?

Wear your Players HUD, accept the Experience permission popup, create a character on the setup screen, then go touch something in the world. That's 90% of it. The rest of this guide explains the depth.

docs.title: First-Time Setup Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the realm! Before you can draw your blade, you need a character on the HUD. Follow these steps to get playing.

docs.title: Manifest Your Gear
docs.text:
Before the Setup HUD can bind a character to you, wear the essentials:

  1. Feudalism 4 Players HUD — attached, not rezzed on the ground.
  2. A Feudalism pouch — extra storage and crafting aggregation (up to two on F4 personal-inventory HUDs).

Some universes also expect a Meter, a drink skin or bucket, and weapon or armor attachments. Ask staff what your region requires.

Touch Options on the Players HUD to open the Setup HUD (or close it).

docs.title: Welcome — choose how to start
docs.text:
If you have no character yet, Setup shows a Welcome screen. You must create a character before you can play. Choose one:

Choice What happens Play as starter Creates a complete starter character and lets you play immediately Create my own Walks you through Universe → Character → Stats → Class → Review → Finish

Nothing is saved for Create my own until you click Finish on Review. Cancel anytime to discard the draft.

docs.title: Starter defaults (Play as starter)
docs.text:
Field Value Universe default (if that universe is locked or at limit, you are sent to Create my own) Name Your Second Life display name, or My First Character Title Blank Gender Other Species Human Mana Off XP 20,000 (+ any species starting bonus) Stats All attributes at 1 Class Adventurer

You can customize name, species, class, and stats anytime later in Setup.

Default universe gated?

If default requires a registration code or you are at the character limit, use Create my own and pick an allowed universe.

Attach the HUD — don't rez it

The HUD only works when worn. If you drop it on the ground it can't track your character.

docs.title: Accept the Experience Permission
docs.text:
The first time you wear the HUD on an Experience-enabled region, Second Life shows a permission popup for the Feudalism Experience. Accept it.

This permission lets the HUD save your XP, vitals, and character data. Without it, nothing you do will be saved. If you declined by accident, detach and re-wear the HUD to get the popup again.

docs.title: Create my own (wizard)
docs.text:
The creation bar shows: Universe → Character → Stats → Class → Review.

Use Cancel creation to discard, or Continue until Finish. On Stats, Reset to defaults undoes XP/AP/stat spends without cancelling creation.

docs.title: 1. Universe
docs.text: Pick your universe (and registration code if staff require one).

docs.title: 2. Character (identity)
docs.text: Enter name (required), optional title, pick gender and species, and whether you want magic (has_mana). Confirm species to receive starting XP and stats.

docs.title: Gender Selector
docs.text: Select your character's physical presentation. Click a portrait in the gallery to view details, then choose Select This Gender.

docs.title: Species Selector
docs.text: Choose your people. Confirming a species sets starting XP (20,000 + species bonus) and resets stats to all 1s for the draft.

docs.title: 3. Stats
docs.text: Spend XP to buy Available Points (AP) and raise stats — or leave them. Without a finished class, raises are capped at 2 until you pick a class. Use Reset to defaults to start the spend over.

docs.title: 4. Class
docs.text: Pick a starting class. During creation, prerequisite career paths are waived for your first class.

docs.title: 5. Review → Finish
docs.text: Confirm gender, species, class, XP, AP, and stats. Click Finish to mint the character into Experience storage and set it active. Cancel creation deletes the draft (nothing was saved mid-wizard).

docs.title: Create another character later
docs.text: Use New Character in Setup. Same wizard: local draft until Finish. Your previous active character stays until the new one is finished.

docs.title: After you have a character
docs.text: Open Setup anytime to edit identity, spend AP, or change class (class changes may cost XP). Touch Sync/Update on the Players HUD after big XP gains or before spending points.

docs.title: Quick-Start Checklist
docs.text: A one-page checklist for new players. Print it, paste it into a notecard, or hand it to a friend.

docs.title: Before you play
docs.text:
• Wear the Feudalism 4 Players HUD (attached, not rezzed).
  • Wear a Feudalism pouch (up to two storage pouches on current HUD builds).
  • Accept the Experience permission popup (required to save anything).
  • Wait a few seconds on first wear — a starter character may be created automatically (default universe).
  • Open the Setup HUD (Options) when you want to change name, species, class, or spend stat points.

docs.title: Everyday actions
docs.text:
• Watch your Health / Stamina / Mana spheres and the XP bar on the HUD.
  • Touch the Options button to edit stats, class, inventory, or game mode.
  • Touch the Sync/Update button after a big XP haul or before spending points.
  • Touch the Rest (heart) button to recover after a fight; Stop Resting before you travel.
  • Touch your pouch (or its HUD action slot) for that bag's menu, or the HUD Inventory button for your character's personal items.

docs.title: Out in the world
docs.text:
• Gather: touch bushes, ore nodes, and trees within range — items go to your personal inventory (overflow to a worn pouch if needed).
  • Hunt: shoot animals with valid arrows or hit them with a drawn Feudalism weapon.
  • Craft: at an oven, anvil, or other station — pick a recipe; it checks and consumes your ingredients.
  • Store: move overflow into a storage chest.
  • Trade: use the Coins menu to pay another player nearby.

docs.title: If something breaks
docs.text:
• HUD and pouch attached? Character loaded? Experience permission accepted?
  • Close enough (most objects: 5–15 m)?
  • Have the right ingredients, spelled correctly (item names are lowercase)?
  • Re-attach the HUD after changing regions, then touch Sync/Update.
  • Still stuck? Tell staff the object's name and what you clicked.

How many pouches?

Feudalism 4 HUD builds support up to two worn storage pouches. A third will not register. Legacy regions may still expect a single old-style pouch — see Personal Inventory & Pouches.

docs.title: What is Feudalism 4?
docs.text: Feudalism 4 (v4.1.0) is a medieval-fantasy roleplay game system for Second Life. Your character has stats, vitals, and skills; you earn experience, fight, hunt, gather, farm, and craft using objects placed around a region. Everything you do as a player runs through one wearable HUD.

docs.title: The core loop
docs.text:
At its heart, Feudalism 4 is a loop you'll repeat in many flavors:

graph LR
  A[Gather raw materials] --> B[Craft into goods]
  B --> C[Use, trade, or sell goods]
  C --> D[Earn XP and coin]
  D --> E[Raise stats and skills]
  E --> A
  • Gather — forage bushes, mine ore, chop wood, hunt animals, milk livestock, draw water.
  • Craft — turn raw materials into food, drink, tools, weapons, and trade goods at workstations.
  • Use & trade — eat food, equip gear, give items to other players, sell for coin.
  • Progress — activities award XP, which becomes Available Points you spend to raise your stats.

docs.title: What runs the game
docs.text: Piece What it is Players HUD A worn attachment that tracks your health, stamina, mana, and XP, runs combat, and talks to world objects. Setup HUD A web panel (opened from the HUD) where you create and edit your character — stats, class, inventory, and game mode. Worn tools Up to two storage pouches, plus optional drink skin and water bucket slotted on the HUD. Liquids and water live in personal inventory; skins and buckets open filtered views. See Personal Inventory. World objects Ovens, anvils, fields, beehives, gatherables, targets, chests, and more — the things you touch to play. The Meter An optional attachment that shows your name, title, and bars in floating text above your head.

docs.title: A roleplay system, not a story
docs.text:
Feudalism 4 provides the mechanics — dice, stats, items, crafting, combat. It does not dictate your region's story, laws, or economy. Each universe (region or group of regions) sets its own rules and can:

  • enable or disable features (combat, mana, currency, specific stations);
  • filter which species, classes, and genders are available;
  • decide consent rules for combat, capture, death, and theft.

When guides disagree, your universe wins

If something here doesn't match what you see in-world, your universe has likely customized it. Ask local staff — they have the final word on rules.

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• Never played? → First-Time Setup
  • Want the short version? → Quick-Start Checklist
  • Curious about the HUD's buttons? → The Players HUD

docs.title: Inventory & Storage
docs.text:
What you carry, where you store it, and the coin in your purse.

  • Personal Inventory & Pouches — HUD inventory per character, up to two worn storage pouches, drink skins, and buckets.
  • Action Slots & Worn Tools — quick-use HUD slots for pouch, bucket, skin, and vessels.
  • Storage Chests — bulk, shareable storage you can rez in the world.
  • Currency & Coins — copper, silver, and gold, and how to pay other players.

Items aren't Second Life inventory

Feudalism items (ore, food, arrows, ingredients) are game data, not objects in your SL inventory. They live in your character's HUD storage and in worn containers — which is what lets stations check and consume them automatically.

Feudalism 4 personal inventory

Current HUD builds store items on the character (pinv_* on the HUD), aggregate up to two worn storage pouches for crafting checks, and route water and brewing liquids through worn bucket/skin doorways — not separate liquid tanks on those attachments.

docs.title: Action Slots & Worn Tools
docs.text: Action slots are quick-use buttons on your HUD that link to tools you wear — pouches, a water bucket, a drink skin, drinking vessels, and buffs — so you can use them without digging through menus.

docs.title: The slot layout
docs.text:
Slots Purpose Weapon slots (2) Primary and secondary weapons — draw/sheath. See Weapons & Armor. Action slots (9, numbered 2–10 on the bar) Worn tools: pouches, bucket, skin, vessels, buffs.

When you attach a recognized tool, the HUD detects it within about one second and lights up a slot. Touch the lit slot to use that tool.

docs.title: Preferred slots
docs.text:
The HUD tries to put common tools in fixed positions (left to right on the bar):

Tool Recognized by object name Preferred slot Slot click opens Storage pouch name contains pouch (PI2 product) 10 That pouch's menu only Water bucket name contains bucket (not a skin) 9 Personal inventory — water filter Drink skin / waterskin / wineskin name contains skin 8 Personal inventory — brewing liquids filter Flasks, horns, mugs, goblets, vials matching keywords next free slot Drink / vessel action for that prop

If the preferred slot is already taken by another tool, the HUD assigns the next free slot to the left (7, 6, 5, …), then to the right if needed.

docs.title: Storage pouches on action slots
docs.text:
• Each PI2 storage pouch registers with the HUD when you attach it and claims one action slot.
  • You may wear at most two storage pouches. A third will not get a slot and should detach automatically.
  • Set the pouch Description to a short label so chat messages and slot clicks name the right bag.
  • Touching a pouch's slot sends openPouchMenu to that pouch only — not the full HUD I menu.

Player steps

  1. Attach your pouch, bucket, or skin as normal Second Life attachments.
  2. Wait about one second after attaching the HUD or a tool (slots light up).
  3. Touch the matching action slot to open or use that tool.
  4. Slot still empty? Re-attach the tool, touch the pouch once to register, or check the object name matches the patterns above.

docs.title: Drink skin and bucket slots
docs.text:
Under the personal inventory model, skins and buckets do not store liquid on the attachment. They register with the HUD so stations know you're wearing them, and the slot opens a filtered inventory view:

  • Slot 8 (skin) — items like wine, must, mash in your character inventory.
  • Slot 9 (bucket) — water in your character inventory (wells fill through the worn bucket).

You can also touch the skin or bucket directly for the same menus.

docs.title: Planning your loadout
docs.text:
Nine action slots sounds like a lot, but weapons and buffs compete with tools. A typical crafter might run:

  • Slot 10 — main pouch (Mining)
  • Slot 9 — water bucket
  • Slot 8 — drink skin
  • Slot 7 — second pouch (Cooking)

Fighters may leave several slots empty for quicker access to weapons and buffs.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Symptom Things to check Slot never lights up HUD attached? Wait ~1 s after wear. Object name matches table above? Third pouch won't stay Two-pouch limit — detach one storage pouch first. Slot click opens wrong pouch Set unique Description labels on each pouch. Bucket/skin slot empty Liquids live in personal inventory — gather or fill at a well first.

See also: Personal Inventory & Pouches · Storage Chests

docs.title: Currency & Coins
docs.text: Many universes use copper, silver, and gold as in-character money. Your balance is stored on your character record — it is not a gatherable world item.

docs.title: Seeing your money
docs.text: Where What you get Setup HUD → Inventory tab A currency bar showing gold / silver / copper. Coins button on the Players HUD A dialog: "You have X gold, Y silver, Z copper." Chat The HUD can announce your balance on request.

docs.title: The Coins menu
docs.text:
Touch the Coins button (if your universe enables currency):

Action What it does Give Coins Pay another player within 10 m — pick their name, the coin type, and an amount. Vault: Deposit / Withdraw Appears when a vault object is nearby. Close Dismiss the menu.

Player steps — pay someone

  1. Stand within 10 m of the other player.
  2. Touch Coins → Give Coins.
  3. Select their name → choose gold / silver / copper → enter an amount.
  4. Confirm. Both balances update.

docs.title: Where coin comes from
docs.text:
Wages, bounties, and market prices are paid out by your universe's economy — often through in-world paychest objects that staff configure, or by admins directly.

Don't try to gather coin

Coins flow only through dedicated currency systems. Gatherable scripts cannot create gold, silver, or copper — those item names are blocked on purpose to protect the economy.

See how admins run the economy in Players, XP & Economy.

docs.title: Personal Inventory & Pouches
docs.text: Your character carries items through the Players HUD — food, ore, honey, arrows, water, wine, and trade goods. World stations read your totals automatically when you craft, and gathered or crafted output lands in your inventory unless a station says otherwise.

docs.title: Two layers of storage
docs.text:
Layer Tied to Where it lives How you open it HUD personal inventory Your character Saved on the HUD (per character) Inventory button on the HUD, or openInventory from worn tools Worn storage pouches (up to 2) The pouch object On each pouch attachment Touch the pouch, or its action slot on the HUD

Stuff is stuff

At the storage layer, iron, water, and wine are all normal item slugs in your personal inventory. A drink skin or water bucket does not hoard liquid on the attachment — it registers with the HUD and opens a filtered view of what's already in your character's inventory. See Drink skins & water buckets below.

docs.title: HUD personal inventory
docs.text:
1. Your personal inventory belongs to your character. Switch from character A to character B and you see B's items, not A's.
  2. Item names are lowercase slugs like iron ore, honey, or flour — not Iron Ore. Spelling and case matter when you type item names.

Open the Inventory prim on your HUD (or use a worn tool's slot — see Action Slots) to browse what you're carrying. From there you can:

Action What it does Consume Eat or drink an edible item. Drop Place items into the world (roleplay responsibly). To Pouch Move items from your HUD inventory into a worn storage pouch (picker if you wear two).

Most crafting stations total personal inventory plus every worn pouch — you don't pick which pouch pays for a recipe.

docs.title: Storage pouches (wear up to two)
docs.text: A storage pouch (or backpack, belt pouch, etc.) is a wearable container with its own item list on the attachment. It expands how much you can carry and behaves like a portable storage chest when rezzed on the ground.

docs.title: Worn limit
docs.text: You may wear at most two storage pouches at once. If you attach a third, the HUD ignores it (no action slot, no pool access) and the pouch is asked to detach. Plan your loadout: two pouches plus a drink skin and bucket still fit other action slots.

docs.title: Label your pouches
docs.text: Set the pouch object's Description field to a short label — for example Cooking or Mining ore. The HUD uses this in chat when you put the pouch on and when you open it from an action slot. Without a description you'll see a pouch ID instead (still works, harder to tell them apart).

docs.title: Pouch menu (touch the pouch)
